summary: Reflecting on Y Combinator's Female Founder Conference, Asparouhova argues that the event succeeded precisely because it avoided centering discussions on gender disadvantages or sexism. Instead, speakers shared substantive advice about building companies, allowing them to come across as multidimensional individuals rather than representatives of a demographic. She critiques the proliferation of identity-based sectors in entrepreneurship, arguing that siloing social impact into categories makes people one-dimensional. The post concludes that true inclusion comes from breaking down sectors rather than building new ones.
key_points: The Female Founder Conference succeeded by focusing on substance (how to build companies) rather than identity-based grievances
Building identity-based sectors (women entrepreneurship, social entrepreneurship, etc.) risks making people one-dimensional and siloing social impact
Exclusion is multidimensional—introversion, non-technical backgrounds, and office geography can matter as much as gender or race
True inclusion comes from breaking down categorical labels, not creating new ones
Meritocratic framing, often criticized, can paradoxically create more authentic and empowering spaces
money_quotes: We are awash in sectors that are ostensibly trying to make business better.
Y Combinator's conference demonstrated what happens when we focus not on building sectors, but rather breaking them down.
Not every female founder wants to be known as a female founder. Not every female VC wants to be known as a female VC.
Some of us just want to be really good at what we do, and recognized for that among our peers: male and female.
Building sectors is dangerous because it makes us one-dimensional.
A straight white male may still feel left out for not being technical, being the quiet one in a room, or being relegated to a corner of the office.
By not affixing the same, flat, 'women X' label onto everyone, the Female Founder Conference allowed everyone in attendance to breathe as themselves.
key_insight: Identity-based sectors intended to promote inclusion can paradoxically flatten individuality, and the most empowering spaces are those that let people transcend their demographic labels.
